segments.s445-g81: همان is a demonstrative/identifying modifier ('that/the same') placed before the nouns. برده here means 'captives/slaves' (a noun) and is simply listed with the following noun via و. بدره‌های درم is a compound phrase: بدره‌های is the plural (badré‑hâ) modifying درم ('dirham/coin'), so 'round dirhams' (badré evokes the full‑moon shape). The whole segment is a nominal list (no finite verb here) describing items.
segments.s445-g82: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing the material or quality (here 'of musk'); the pattern ز X و ز Y repeats to list two materials ('of musk and of camphor'). وز in the manuscript is an awkward fused form here and does not carry its literal senses; بیش و کم is an idiomatic pair meaning 'more and less' or 'in greater and lesser amounts'. Together the segment qualifies the previously listed items (they are scented/associated with musk and camphor, and come in larger and smaller quantities).
segments.s445-g83: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('which/that'). رستم is the subject. فراز is an adverb/adpositional element meaning 'up/on high' placed before the verb for poetic word‑order. آورید is an older poetic past form of آوردن ('to bring') meaning 'brought'. آن is the pronoun/object 'that' and به رنج is an adverbial phrase 'with hardship/effort' that modifies the action.
segments.s445-g84: ز here means 'from' and introduces the sources ('from kings and rebels'). شاهان و گردنکشان are coordinated nouns (kings; the rebellious/proud ones). یافت is the past tense of یافتن ('obtained/found') and takes گنج ('treasure') as its object; the verb precedes the object here due to poetic inversion but functions as a normal past action.
segments.s445-g85: همه quantifies زابلستان ('all Zabulistan'). به تاراج داد is a light verbal phrase: به تاراج ('to pillage') + داد (past of دادن 'gave') — a common poetic idiom meaning 'he gave over to pillage' or 'he laid waste/plundered'. Thus the clause says the whole region was delivered to plunder (caused to be pillaged).
segments.s445-g86: مهان را uses را, the definite object/contrast marker, to mark/foreground the recipients 'the guests' (مهان). همه modifies the following nouns ('all the badreh and crown'). بدره and تاج are the goods given; داد is the past verb 'gave'. Word order here is topical/poetic but the sense is straightforward: 'to the guests he gave every badreh and crown.'
segments.s445-g73: ز (z) is the poetic preposition 'from'. ایوان (ivān) means 'porch/iwan' and is simply the place from which the action begins. دستان here functions as 'the hands/attendants' (an older, somewhat figurative use meaning the men who work for someone) and سام is the proper name 'Sam'; سوار follows as a noun 'rider/horseman' describing the persons — the sequence gives a compact, slightly telegraphic phrase 'from the iwan Sam's attendants and riders'.
segments.s445-g74: شتر (shotor) 'camel' is the object being loaded; بارها (bārhā) here is not the adverb 'many times' but plural/collective 'loads, burdens' (i.e. pack-lots). برنهادند is past plural 'they lifted/placed upon' (بر + نهادند, an older past form meaning 'set on/loaded'). The final بار (bār) repeats the idea 'load/cargo' as the direct object; the clause therefore says they placed loads onto the pack-camels.
segments.s445-g75: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' or here functioning like 'in/with'. دینار is 'dinar/coin' (money). وز in classical Persian often functions as a connective (an archaic variant of و 'and'), but that specific sense isn't listed in the supplied sense set, so it is treated as an archaic conjunction here. نابسود in context means 'not withheld / not sparing' (a negative adjective describing that they did not stint on money and jewels) rather than the literal modern senses given.
segments.s445-g76: ز again is the preposition introducing the next class of items ('from/on'). تخت is 'throne' (here the platform or seat of state). وز (again) functions as the archaic conjunction 'and/with' (not listed among the supplied senses). گستردنی is a deverbal adjective/nominal form meaning 'that which is to be spread' (cloths, carpets, or trappings to be spread out), and هرچ بود is 'whatever there was' with بود as simple past 'was' — together: 'and whatever could be spread on the throne was there.'
segments.s445-g77: ز marks the list 'of/from' these things. سیمینه is an adjective 'silvery' (i.e. silver articles). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. تاجهای is the plural 'crowns'. به here introduces the material or ornamenting ('in/with'); in older usage به can mean 'made of' or 'with the quality of', so تاجهای به زر = 'crowns with/of gold'.
segments.s445-g78: ز begins another item-class 'of/and'. زرینه is 'golden' (an adjective). و is 'and'. گوشوار is 'earring/ornament', and the following و links the final item کمر which here means 'girdle, belt' (a decorative belt), not the anatomical 'waist'. The segment is a simple noun list of jewelry and belt.
segments.s445-g79: از is the preposition 'from/of'. اسپان is the plural 'horses/steeds'. تازی here qualifies them as 'Arabian' or specifically 'Arabian horse(s)' (an epithet commonly used in Persian for fine horses). به is used instrumentally/qualifyingly here as 'with' (archaic usage), marking the following adjective; زرین is 'golden'. ستام in this context denotes some piece of tack or trappings (a harness/saddle-ornament) associated with the horses; the exact lexical sense is archaic/technical and not provided in the supplied sense list, so its sense is left unspecified.
segments.s445-g80: ز again 'of/and'. شمشیر is 'sword'. هندی qualifies it 'Indian' (i.e. made in India or Indian style). به is again the instrumental/associative 'with' (archaic usage), زرین 'golden' modifies نیام, and نیام is 'scabbard/sheath'. The phrase describes Indian swords accompanied by golden scabbards.
segments.s445-g49: فرستاده را marks the messenger as the definite direct object with را after the noun; the clause ends with داد, the simple past 'gave'. اسپ و دینار is a coordinated object ('a horse and dinars/coins'). Note اسپ is an older/poetic form of modern اسب.
segments.s445-g50: ز هرگونه‌ای چیز بسیار داد is an additional clause repeating the giving: ز (from/of/regarding) + هرگونه‌ای 'of every kind' qualifies چیز 'things'; بسیار modifies چیز as 'many/much'. داد is again the past 'gave'. Word order places the qualifier phrase before the verb, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s445-g51: چو is the temporal 'when'; این مایه‌ور is 'this rich/wealthy one' (مایه‌ور = wealthy). پیش بهمن means 'in the presence of Bahman' or 'before Bahman' (پیش + personal name). رسید is the past 'arrived/came'.
segments.s445-g52: ز دستان is a poetic phrase roughly 'in words/tales' or 'by way of narrative' (ز = 'from/of' and دستان here = 'tales, stories'). بگفت is the past 'he said/related'; آنچ is the relative 'that which'; دید و شنید are coordinated past verbs 'saw and heard' — together: 'he told that which he saw and heard.'
segments.s445-g53: چو introduces the temporal clause 'when'; بشنید is the past 'heard' with ازو preceding the person/thing heard 'from him/of it'. بهمن نیک‌بخت is the name with an epithet 'Bahman the fortunate' in apposition; Persian allows the agent (بهمن) after the verb in such constructions.
segments.s445-g54: نپذرفت is the past negative 'did not accept / refused' with پوزش ('apology, excuse') as its object. برآشفت is an intransitive past 'he flew into rage / became angry' and سخت is an adverbial intensifier 'severely/strongly' modifying the anger.
segments.s445-g55: به شهر اندر آمد is a locative motion phrase 'entered into the city' (به + شهر + اندر + آمد). دلی پر ز درد is a postverbal description: دلی (here functioning as 'his heart') + پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of/from' + درد 'pain/sorrow' — 'with a heart full of pain.'
segments.s445-g56: سری پر ز کین is 'a head full of kins' — سری here = 'head', پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from', کین = 'vengeance/hatred'. لب پر از باد سرد follows in parallel: لب 'lip' + پر 'full' + از 'with/from' + باد 'wind' + سرد 'cold', describing his chilled/pale lips; the two phrases jointly characterize his physical and mental state.
segments.s445-g25: همانا is an emphatic particle meaning 'truly' or 'verily'. شنیدی is the second-person singular past of شنیدن ('to hear') and introduces the embedded clause with که; سام سوار is a noun phrase ('Sam the horseman') where سوار is a noun qualifier following the name.
segments.s445-g26: This is the content clause introduced by که: 'به مردی چه کرد اندران روزگار' = 'what he did to a man in those days.' به + noun marks the indirect object ('to a man'). چه is the interrogative 'what', کرد is the past tense ('did'), اندران is a locative/archaic adverbial 'therein/among them' used with روزگار to mean 'in those times' or 'then', and روزگار means 'time/age'.
segments.s445-g27: چنین ('thus') frames the action. تا here functions as a subordinating 'until'; the temporal phrase 'به هنگام رستم' ('at the time of Rostam') is placed before the verb. رسید is the past tense 'arrived' or 'came', so the clause means 'until the time Rostam arrived.'
segments.s445-g28: که introduces the following temporal/result clause ('when/that'). شمشیر تیز is a noun + adjective 'sharp sword'. از میان is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the midst', and برکشید is the simple past third-person form meaning 'he drew/raised' — here specifically 'drew (a sword)'. The subject is Rostam carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s445-g29: به پیش نیاکان تو در is an idiomatic locative phrase meaning 'in the presence of your ancestors' or 'before your ancestors' (به پیش + noun phrase). در here reinforces the locative sense. چه کرد is the interrogative 'what did (he) do' with کرد in the past tense.
segments.s445-g30: This is a circumstantial phrase modifying what was done: به مردی = 'to a man' (indirect object). به هنگام is the temporal prepositional phrase 'at the time of'; ننگ و نبرد is a coordinated noun phrase 'shame (disgrace) and battle', specifying the situation/time.
segments.s445-g31: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that same'. کهتر here functions as a noun/adjective 'the lesser/younger one' or 'inferior'. دایگان تو = 'your wet-nurse/caretaker' (possessive follows the noun). بود is the past tense 'was/were' (classical poetry often uses singular agreement even for coordinated subjects).
segments.s445-g32: به لشکر = 'in/at the army' (locative), ز is the poetic preposition 'from/among', پرمایگان تو = 'your great ones' or 'your nobles' (possessive after the noun), and بود again is the past copula 'was/were' indicating their presence among the host.
segments.s445-g17: تو (subject) + بودی (past of بودن) gives a simple past clause 'you were'. The prepositional phrase 'به نیک و بد اندر میان' follows and is poetic: به + noun pair 'نیک و بد' (good and bad) with اندر میان meaning 'in the middle/among'; word order (subject — verb — prepositional phrase) is typical of Ferdowsi's elevated, somewhat inverted style.
segments.s445-g18: ز من (from me) marks the source. سود دیدی is a past affirmative clause 'you saw/received benefit'; ندیدی is the same past verb with negative prefix ن- ('you did not see'), and زیان is its object 'harm/loss'. The two clauses are paralleled without a conjunction: 'you saw benefit from me, (and) you did not see loss.'
segments.s445-g19: نپیچید is the past negative of پیچیدن here meaning 'did not turn aside / did not deviate'. The proper name رستم follows the verb (verb–subject order). ز فرمان اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from his command', where اوی is an archaic third‑person possessive ('his').
segments.s445-g20: دلش is دل + possessive suffix ش 'his heart'. بسته بودی is a past stative 'was bound/tied' (بوده/بودی used as past copula), and به پیمان اوی is a dative/locative phrase 'to/by the pledge of his' meaning 'bound by his covenant' — an idiom for loyalty.
segments.s445-g21: پدرت is پدر + second‑person suffix ت 'your father'. The string 'آن گرانمایه شاه بزرگ' is an appositive nominal group: آن (that) + adjective گرانمایه (esteemed) + noun شاه (king) + adjective بزرگ (great), all describing the father in lofty, archaic diction.
segments.s445-g22: زمانش (his time) with possessive suffix, بیامد is the past 'came' ('his time came' = 'when his hour arrived'). بدان شد is the phrase 'thereupon he became' (بدان = 'in/thereon'), followed by سترگ as the resulting predicate 'mighty/great' in elevated diction. (Note: دان and سترگ are archaic/poetic items here; the sequence means 'when his time came, he became great.')
segments.s445-g23: به بیشه درون is a locative phrase 'in/into the inner grove/thicket' (به + بیشه + درون). شیر و نر اژدها lists two creatures in coordination: 'a lion and a male dragon' (نر qualifies اژدها as 'male'). The sequence is nominative/nominal, setting up the subjects for the following clause.
segments.s445-g24: ز چنگ زمانه is a genitive phrase 'from the claw/grip of Time/Fate' (ز = from; چنگ = claw/talon; زمانه = time/fate). نیابد is the negative third‑person singular of یافتن ('does not attain/find'), and رها is the object 'release/freedom' — together 'they do not obtain release from Time's claw', i.e. they cannot escape fate.
segments.s445-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a past action; آمد is the past-tense verb 'came'. به نزدیکی is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the nearness (of)', with نزدیکی 'nearness' following به. هیرمند is the place name (Helmand) governed by the preposition.
segments.s445-g2: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite/agent noun 'a messenger' (participle-derived noun) and functions as the object/actor to be chosen; برگزید is the past tense 'chose/selected' (transitive past); ارجمند is a postpositive adjective 'noble/esteemed' describing the messenger.
segments.s445-g3: فرستاد is a past-tense verb 'sent/dispatched' (the direct object is implied from the previous clause). نزدیک here modifies the following noun group as 'near/close to'; دستان literally 'hands' is used metaphorically for 'those close to (Sam)', and سام is the owner in this genitive relationship (دستانِ سام). Note the compact, poetic ordering without an explicit ezafe.
segments.s445-g4: بدادش is a verbal phrase with the enclitic -ش 'to him' meaning 'he gave him' (poetic/contracted form linking the action to the recipient). ز introduces the source/qualification 'of/from'; هر گونه 'every kind' is a fixed expression; چندی پیام 'several/messages' places the quantifier (چندی) before پیام. The whole phrase describes giving messages of various kinds.
segments.s445-g5: چنین گفت is an idiom 'he thus said' with چنین 'thus' placed before the verb. کز is the archaic subordinating particle (equivalent to که) meaning 'that' or 'because of' depending on context; here it introduces a causal/genitive phrase 'from the hatred of Esfandiyar' (کین اسفندیار).
segments.s445-g6: مرا (here split as م + را in the manuscript) is the accusative 'me' (را is the object marker); تلخ شد is a two-part predicate 'became bitter' (adjective + شد, past-tense result); در جهان روزگار is a locative/temporal phrase 'in the world/time' qualifying how life has become bitter.
segments.s445-g7: هم is an additive adverb 'also' placed before the cause phrase. از کین 'from/because of the hatred' sets the reason. نوش‌آذر و مهر نوش are two compound proper names joined by و; in classical style the name elements like نوش appear as lexical morphemes ('nush' = 'drink/delight') but here they operate as parts of names.
segments.s445-g8: دو is the numeral 'two' repeated for rhetorical emphasis; شاه گرامی is 'honored kings' with the adjective after the noun (classical/poetic ordering); دو فرخ سروش apposes a second epithet, 'two fortunate Soroush' (فرخ 'fortunate' modifying سروش). The sequence names and epithets are stacked for emphasis.
segments.s445-g41: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a small emphatic/temporal particle ('like this / at this point') modifying the following clause. بینی تو places the verb بینی ('you see') before the pronoun تو (poetic inversion of verb + subject) and پیکار ما is the direct object 'our battle' (پیکار = battle, ما = our).
segments.s445-g42: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase ('well / carefully') where به functions as an adverbial marker and خوبی supplies the quality. براندیشی is a 2nd-person verb form meaning 'you should consider / ponder' (subjunctive/imperative flavor). از کار ما is a prepositional complement 'about our affair' (از = about/concerning; کار ما = our affair).
segments.s445-g43: بیایی is an exhortative/imperative 'come' (used to introduce a pleading or command). ز دل is a prepositional phrase 'from the heart' (ز = from), کینه بیرون کنی is the action clause where کینه 'hatred' is the object, بیرون 'out' is an adverb, and کنی is the 2nd-person verb 'you make/remove' (subjunctive/imperative sense: 'do away with').
segments.s445-g44: به مهر is a prepositional phrase 'with love / by kindness' (به + مهر). اندرین کشور 'in this country/land' uses اندرین ('in these / in this') before کشور. افسون کنی is a verbal phrase where افسون 'charm/enchantment' is the object and کنی is again the 2nd-person verb 'you do' (i.e. 'you enchant' / 'you bewitch' or figuratively 'you win over').
segments.s445-g45: همه quantifies the following nouns ('all'). The sequence گنج فرزند و دینار lists nouns 'treasure, (a) child/descendant, and dinars' with سام placed after them as the possessor ('of Sam'); classical Persian often places the possessor after the possessed without an explicit ezafe. The coordination is simple listing: 'all the treasure, offspring, and money of Sam.'
segments.s445-g46: کمرهای is the plural 'belts/girdles' (کمر + plural suffix های). زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying کمرهای. The conjunction و links a second nominal phrase زرین ستام where زرین again 'golden' modifies ستام; ستام here is a noun 'praise' (poetic use), so the phrase reads 'golden girdles and golden praise.'
segments.s445-g47: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آیی is 2nd-person present 'you come'. به پیش تو is 'into your presence / before you' (به + پیش + pronoun). آرم is a 1st-person verb 'I place / I lay down / I rest' (here idiomatically 'I present/offer'), and همه is the object 'everything.' Note the verb–subject/pronoun ordering and the dropped copula in poetic register.
segments.s445-g48: تو شاهی is a predicate nominal: تو (you) + شاهی (kingship/you are king) with the copula omitted (typical in Persian). و links the next clause. گردنکشانت is a plural possessive 'your rebels' (گردنکش = rebellious ones, -انت = your), and رمه is a predicate noun 'a flock / throng' — again with an omitted copula; the phrase conveys 'you are king, and your rebels are a mere herd.'
segments.s445-g57: پذیره شدش is a passive/periphrastic construction: پذیره (reception/acceptance) + شد (became/happened) with the enclitic -ش (= 'him'), so literally 'a reception happened to him' = 'he was received'. In Ferdowsīan word order the verbal phrase comes before its agents, so the following names (زال سام) are the recipients/agents in apposition; سوار is an appositive/epithet for سام ('Sam, the rider').
segments.s445-g58: هم از سیستان آنک بد نامدار is a descriptive tag attached to the preceding names. هم = 'also/too'; از سیستان = 'from Sistan' (prepositional phrase); آنک is a relative/pronominal connector ('which/who') introducing the qualifier بد نامدار = 'bad‑named' (i.e. ill‑famed/notorious). The whole phrase means 'also from Sistan, who was of ill repute.'
segments.s445-g59: چو آمد = 'when he came' (چو = when; آمد = came). به نزدیک بهمن فراز is a prepositional/locative phrase: به + نزدیک = 'to/near', followed by بهمن (proper name) + فراز ('height/summit'), so 'when he came near Bahman's summit/height.'
segments.s445-g60: پیاده شد = 'he dismounted' (پیاده = on foot / dismounted; شد = became/did). از باره = 'because of the matter / on that occasion' (بارِه/باره used poetically as 'occasion'). بردش نماز is an older, slightly inverted construction: برد (here with enclitic -ش = 'him') = 'took him' and نماز is the thing that 'took' him; i.e. 'prayer seized him' → 'he fell to prayer' or 'he began to pray.' The verb with enclitic follows the noun acting as its logical subject, a poetic inversion.
segments.s445-g61: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' though the supplied sense list was nominal). هنگامی/هنگام بخشایش است = 'it is the time of forgiveness' (هنگام = time; بخشایش = forgiveness; است = is). The clause is direct speech introduction.
segments.s445-g62: ز دل‌درد و کین = 'from (or away from) heartache and hatred/vengeance' (ز is the poetic form of از = 'from'; دل‌درد = sorrow/heartache; کین = grudge/vengeance). روز پالایش است = 'is a day of purification' (روز = day; پالایش = purification/cleansing; است = is). Together: 'this day is for cleansing away heartache and vengeance.'
segments.s445-g63: ازان نیکویها = 'from those good/virtuous things' (ازان = 'of those / from that set'; نیکویها = plural 'good things/deeds'). که ما کرده‌ایم is a relative clause 'which we have done' (که = which; ما کرده‌ایم = we have done). The whole means 'of those good deeds which we have done.'
segments.s445-g64: ترا is written here as the sequence ت + را (old/poetic spelling): ت = 2nd‑person object element + را = definite object marker, so ترا = 'you' (object). در جوانی = 'in youth' (during your youth). بپرورده‌ایم = 'we have reared / we have nurtured' (perfect tense, first‑person plural). Together: 'we have reared you in your youth.'
segments.s445-g33: به زاری is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'in/with lamentation'; کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'; رستم is the subject; اندرگذشت is an archaic past verb meaning 'passed away / died' (third person singular). Word order is topical adverbial → time → subject → verb, typical in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s445-g34: همه modifies زابلستان as 'the whole/entire Zābulistān'; پرآشوب is a predicate adjective 'tumultuous'; گشت is a past/resultative verb 'became' (third person singular). The clause means 'the whole Zābulistān became tumultuous.'
segments.s445-g35: شب و روز forms a temporal phrase 'night and day'; هستم is first‑person present 'I am' (here used as a stative copula); ز before درد marks cause/reason 'because of / from the pain'; درد پسر is a genitive construction 'the pain of (my) son'. Overall: 'I am (so) night and day because of my son's pain.'
segments.s445-g36: پر از ... is the repeating pattern 'full of ...': پر از آب‌دیده = 'full of water/tears' (i.e. tearful), and پر از خاک سر = 'full of dust on the head' (a mourning gesture). Note the adjective پر precedes از + noun in classical style.
segments.s445-g37: خروشان و جوشان are vivid participial/adjectival descriptors 'roaring and seething' describing emotional turmoil; دل پر ز درد uses the same 'پر ز' pattern to mean 'a heart full of pain' (ز marks the cause). The adjectives and the genitive phrase cluster to describe inner emotional state.
segments.s445-g38: دو رخ literally 'two cheeks' (poetic for the two sides of the face); زرد here means 'pale/wan'; شده is the resultative 'has become'; لاژورد is a color noun 'lapis-lazuli / deep azure' used poetically for bluish lips. The clause describes the visible, physical signs of grief.
segments.s445-g39: که introduces a subordinate optative/wish-clause here; نفرین برو باد is an older optative formula 'may a curse go (to)...' (برو + باد forming 'may it go'); کو را ز پای is a poetic relative/target phrase: کو (archaic 'who/whosoever') + را (object/topic marker) + ز پای 'from the feet' (i.e. 'off his feet' = caused him to fall). The verb is continued in the next segment.
segments.s445-g40: فگند is the past verb 'cast/struck down' completing the relative clause started earlier; و links an additional target: بر آنکس 'upon that person' (بر marks the target of the curse), که introduces the relative 'who', and بد رهنمای is a nominal phrase 'bad guide' (i.e. one who gave bad counsel). The construction places the curse on both actors.
segments.s445-g65: This is a sequence of two imperatives joined by و. ببخشای is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person-singular imperative of بخشیدن ('forgive'). کار گذشته is the object phrase 'past deed(s)' (کار = 'deed'), and مگوی is the negative imperative ('do not speak/tell'), so the clause literally orders: 'Forgive, and do not speak of past deeds.'
segments.s445-g66: هنر جوی is another imperative: هنر (art/skill) as object and جوی (imperative 'seek') = 'seek skill.' وز here is an archaic poetic contraction meaning 'و ز' ('and from' / 'and, of'), linking to the following nominal phrase. کشتگان کین is a genitive grouping 'the vengeance of the slain' (کشتگان = 'the slain', کین = 'vengeance'), and مجوی is the negative imperative ('do not seek').
segments.s445-g67: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/for'), here meaning 'for/because.' پیش تو = 'before you / in your presence.' دستان سام is a poetic noun phrase where دستان (literally 'hands' or 'feats') is attached to the name سام (ezāfe is omitted in verse), giving 'Sam's hands/feats'; سوار is an appositive 'rider/horseman' names Sam's role. The whole phrase supplies the subject of the following verb.
segments.s445-g68: بیامد is simple past 'came.' چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' and modifies the following adjectival phrase. خوار is a predicate adjective 'despised/humbled,' and با + دستوار is a prepositional phrase 'with دستوار' where دستوار (hand‑like + -وار) is an adjectival/nominal form meaning 'steady/firm of hand.' The clause describes the manner in which Sam the rider arrived.
segments.s445-g69: برآشفت is a past intransitive verb 'became angry/was enraged.' بهمن is a proper name (here the person Bahman). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and takes گفتار اوی ('his speech') as its object: گفتار = 'speech/utterance' and اوی is the possessive 'his.' The whole clause: 'Bahman was angered by his speech.'
segments.s445-g70: چنان 'so/thus' modifies the following copular clause. سست شد is 'became weak' (سست = adjective 'weak', شد = past of 'become'). تیز بازار اوی is the subject phrase 'his brisk/keen market' (تیز بازار = 'active/brisk market', اوی = 'his'). Word order places the adverb first for emphasis: 'Thus his brisk market became weak.'
segments.s445-g71: هم‌اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at the same time' (هم‌اندر = 'together/at once', زمان = 'time'). پای کردش به بند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: پای ('foot') + کردش (کرد + ش, 'they made him/they did to him') + به بند ('into fetters' — به + بند = 'into a bond/fetter'). The whole means 'they put his feet in fetters' / 'they fettered his feet.'
segments.s445-g72: ز is the preposition 'from.' دستور و گنجور are coordinated sources ('دستور' here = 'command/authority' and 'گنجور' = 'treasury/treasury-house'). نشنید پند is the negative past 'he did not hear/heed advice' (نشنید = 'did not hear', پند = 'advice/counsel'). Grammatically the phrase means he paid no heed to counsel coming from the authorities and the treasury.
segments.s445-g9: ز + دل = 'from the heart' (ز is the poetic form of از). کین دیرینه is a post‑nominal noun phrase 'long‑standing hatred'. بیرون کنیم is the verb phrase: کنیم is the 1st‑person‑plural subjunctive/hortative ('let us/we will'), so the clause means 'let us drive out / remove'. Word order: prepositional phrase → object → verb.
segments.s445-g10: همه بوم زابل = 'the whole land of Zabul' (همه modifies بوم; بوم زابل = Zabul's soil). پر از خون کنیم is a predicate: پر از = 'full of' and خون is the filling noun; کنیم again is hortative/optative 'let us make' or 'we will make'. The literal order is 'whole land Zabul full of blood make'.
segments.s445-g11: فرستاده آمد = 'the messenger came' (subject + past tense آمد). به زابل is a locative 'to Zabul'. بگفت is the classical past 'he said' (ب‑ prefix is literary). Together: 'A messenger came to Zabul and said'.
segments.s445-g12: دل زال = 'Zal's heart' (possessor after possessed in Persian poetry). با درد و غم گشت جفت is literal 'became paired/joined with pain and sorrow'; گشت is past of شدن 'became', and جفت here is used metaphorically to mean 'joined/paired' — i.e. 'was filled/overwhelmed by'.
segments.s445-g13: چنین داد پاسخ = 'thus gave an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). که‌گر is the archaic/poetic conditional particle 'که گر' = 'if'. شهریار is the subject of the following conditional clause: 'if the king...'. The phrase introduces a reported conditional.
segments.s445-g14: براندیشد is the 3rd‑person (subjunctive/optative) of اندیشیدن 'to reflect/consider' (literary form with prefix بر‑). از کار اسفندیار = 'about / concerning the affair of Esfandiyar' (از marks the topic/concern). Together: 'if the king contemplates Esfandiyar's matter'.
segments.s445-g15: بداند is a literary/hortative or jussive form of دانستن here used in reported speech ('let him know' / 'he should know' / 'know that...'). که introduces the content clause. آن بودنی is a compound: آن 'that' + بودنی 'to‑be / intended' (a deverbal adjective meaning 'that which is to be' or 'the intended'), and کار بود is the predicate 'was a task/affair' (بود is the copula). The clause asserts that the forthcoming/considered action is (or was) a serious affair.
segments.s445-g16: مرا زان سخن = 'to me, from that speech' (مرا = 'to me' / experiencer; here split in the annotation as م + را). دل پرآزار بود = 'the heart was full of pain / greatly distressed' (پرآزار 'very troublesome/distressing', بود past copula). Together: 'to me, that speech made my heart very distressed.'

sentences.s445-s41: Those same captives and the round dirhams, (perfumed) with musk and with camphor, in greater and lesser amounts.
sentences.s445-s42: which Rostam, with hardship, brought up on high; from kings and rebels he obtained treasure.
sentences.s445-s43: He gave (or yielded) all Zabulistan to pillage; to the guests he gave every badreh and crown.
sentences.s445-s37: From the portico Sam's attendants and horsemen lifted loads onto the camels.
sentences.s445-s38: They spared neither dinars nor jewels; and whatever there was to be spread on the throne they provided.
sentences.s445-s39: Of silver and crowns of gold, and golden earrings and belts.
sentences.s445-s40: Arabian steeds with golden trappings, and Indian swords with golden scabbards.
sentences.s445-s25: He gave the messenger a horse and dinars; he gave many things of every kind.
sentences.s445-s26: When this wealthy man came before Bahman, he recounted what he had seen and heard.
sentences.s445-s27: When Bahman the fortunate heard of it, he would not accept the excuse and became greatly angry.
sentences.s445-s28: He entered the city with a heart full of pain; his head full of hatred, his lips filled with the cold wind.
sentences.s445-s13: Truly you have heard what Sam the horseman did to a man in those times.
sentences.s445-s14: Thus it continued until Rostam arrived, when he drew his sharp sword from the midst.
sentences.s445-s15: What did he do to a man in the presence of your ancestors, at a time of shame and battle?
sentences.s445-s16: That same inferior (or younger one) and your wet-nurse were in the army among your great men.
sentences.s445-s9: You stood in the midst of good and evil; you gained benefit from me and did not suffer loss.
sentences.s445-s10: Rostam did not turn aside from his command; his heart was bound to his pledge.
sentences.s445-s11: Your father, that noble great king — when his time came, he became mighty then.
sentences.s445-s12: In the inner thicket a lion and a male dragon — from the clutch of Time they do not find release.
sentences.s445-s1: When he came near the Helmand, he chose a noble messenger.
sentences.s445-s2: He sent (him) to Sam's close attendants and gave him messages of every kind.
sentences.s445-s3: He said thus that, because of Esfandiyar's hatred, my life has become bitter in the world.
sentences.s445-s4: Also, from the hatred of Nush-Azar and Mehr-Nush — two honored kings, two fortunate Soroush.
sentences.s445-s21: If you see our battle like this, consider our affair carefully.
sentences.s445-s22: Come, drive the hatred out of your heart; with affection, enchant this land.
sentences.s445-s23: All Sam's treasure, offspring and dinars — golden girdles and golden praise.
sentences.s445-s24: When you come before me I will lay everything before you: you are king, and your rebellious ones are a flock.
sentences.s445-s29: Zal received him; Sam, the rider from Sistan as well, who was ill‑famed.
sentences.s445-s30: When he came near Bahman's height, he dismounted; overcome by the occasion, he fell to prayer.
sentences.s445-s31: He said to him, 'This is the time for forgiveness; this day is for purging (away) heartache and vengeance.'
sentences.s445-s32: From those good deeds we have performed, we raised you in your youth.
sentences.s445-s17: Rostam now died amid lamentation; all of Zābulistān was thrown into turmoil.
sentences.s445-s18: Night and day I am, from the anguish for my son — my eyes brimming with tears and my head covered with dust.
sentences.s445-s19: Roaring and seething, with a heart full of sorrow, his two cheeks were pale and his lips had turned lapis-blue.
sentences.s445-s20: May a curse fall on whoever cast him from his feet, and on that man who was a bad guide.
sentences.s445-s33: Forgive, and speak not of past deeds; seek skill, and seek not vengeance for the slain.
sentences.s445-s34: For before you came Sam the rider, so humbled and yet with a steady hand.
sentences.s445-s35: Bahman was angered by his speech; so his once-brisk market became weak.
sentences.s445-s36: At the same time they fettered his feet; he heeded no counsel from the commanders or the treasury.
sentences.s445-s5: Let us drive the long‑standing hatred out of the heart, and make all the land of Zabul run with blood.
sentences.s445-s6: A messenger came to Zabul and said, 'Zal's heart became full of pain and sorrow.'
sentences.s445-s7: He answered thus: 'If the king reflects on Esfandiyar's affair...'
sentences.s445-s8: Know that that intended deed is a task; for me, that speech made my heart very distressed.
